mappings.rationaleAI: Confucius (551-479 BC) as the founder whose teachings established Confucianism directly addresses the origin in the life and teachings of Confucius (B.1.b) and Confucianism broadly (B).
Laozi as the legendary author of the Tao Te Ching directly addresses the origin and early developments of Taoism, including the first evidence of Lao-tzu's teachings (C.1.a) and Taoism broadly (C).

The Tang dynasty's Confucian-Buddhist-Daoist religious synthesis connects to the dominance of the Buddhist Way and rise of Taoist-inspired cults (A.2.c). Confucianism's continued role during Buddhist ascendancy (B.1.f) and developments in Taoism under the Tang (C.1.d) are directly addressed.

Confucianism with its core concepts of ren, li, yi, Neo-Confucianism, and influence across China, Korea, Japan, and Vietnam comprehensively addresses Confucianism (B), including its history (B.1), literature (B.2), and nature as religion and philosophy (B.3).

Shinto as Japan's indigenous polytheistic and animistic religion centered on kami directly addresses Shinto: the Way of the Gods (E.2), including beliefs, rituals, mythology from the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, and early clan religion before the 6th century (E.1.a).

Taoism organized around the Tao, wu wei, and practices including meditation and internal alchemy comprehensively addresses Taoism (C), its history from Warring States through Han dynasty onward (C.1), Taoist literature (C.2), and Taoism as religion and philosophy (C.3).
Eastern philosophy's Chinese traditions including Confucianism, Taoism, and Legalism connect to Confucianism as a religion and philosophy (B.3) and Taoism as a religion and philosophy (C.3).

The Han dynasty's Confucian court ideology and establishment of Confucianism as state orthodoxy directly address the formulation of the Great Tradition (A.2.b), Confucianism as state orthodoxy of the Han Empire (B.1.d), and Taoist developments during the Han period (C.1.b).
The history of religion covering East Asian religions provides broad context for Chinese religion (A), Confucianism (B), Taoism (C), Korean religions (D), and Japanese religions (E).
Buddhism's historical spread to East Asia connects to the dominance of the Buddhist Way in China (A.2.c) and the introduction of Buddhism to Japan in the early historic period (E.1.b).
Mahayana's contemporary forms in Chinese, Korean, Japanese, and Vietnamese Buddhism connect to the Buddhist Way in China (A.2.c) and the impact of Chinese influences on Shinto (E.1.b).

Chinese folk religion's veneration of ancestors, local deities, and spirits directly addresses the distinction between folk and literate religions in China (A.1), ritual practices (A.4), and Chinese mythology (A.6).
East Asia's Confucianism, Buddhism, Taoism, and Shinto provide geographic and cultural context for all sections of indigenous religions of East Asia.

Mao's Communist revolution and Cultural Revolution represent the effects of nationalism and secularism on Chinese religious traditions (A.2.d) and the impact of political developments on Confucian ideology (B.1.h).
The Tale of Genji's Buddhist themes of impermanence within Heian court culture connect to Japanese religious art and symbolism (E.3).
Asian history covering classical dynasties and medieval kingdoms provides historical context for Chinese religion (A), Korean religions (D), and Japanese religions (E).
South Korea's ancient Korean kingdoms and religious traditions including Buddhism and Neo-Confucianism connect to the religions of Korea (D).
Ancient East Asian history provides context for the emergence of Chinese religious traditions (A.2).
Chinese Confucian, Daoist, and Buddhist philosophical thought connects to Confucianism as philosophy (B.3) and Taoism as philosophy (C.3).
East Asian visual arts traditions including Chinese scholar-painting connect to Chinese religious symbolism (A.5) and Japanese religious art and symbolism (E.3).
Christianity as a Western religion affecting Chinese familial and social systems connects to the modern period's effects of Western religions (A.2.d).
The early modern period's globalization and secularization connect to the modern effects on Chinese religious traditions (A.2.d) and the Tokugawa era in Japan (E.1.c).
